title: host.containers.internal resolves to podman3 ip instead of wsl 2 adapter. Host applications inaccessable.
body:
### Issue Description

Host applications on Windows 11 with WSL2 are inaccessable due to podman internal resolver that map host-gateway to podman3 ethernet adapter ip within the WSL2, which points to 10.89.0.1 not to the WSL2 / eth0 adapater ip  172.23.137.x in my case. Manually setting host.containers.internal to the adapter ip with extra_hosts in the compose file resolves the problem. However, the adapter ip might be subject to change. Therefore a static ip is not a good idea in a dynamic setup.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
Just use the default podman-machine-default vm on a fresh installed wsl 2 in windows 11.


### Describe the results you received

timeout trying to access host application on 10.89.0.1 ip address

### Describe the results you expected

host-gateway resolves to the right ip of the WSL 2 network adapter 172.x.x.x to access Windows 11 application on ports as intended.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Windows 11 25H2 26200.8037 Windows Feature Experience Pack 1000.26100.300.0

### Additional information

Issue persistant.

Found Workaround. Create a script which updates "host_containers_internal_ip" automatically and add it to system startup:
`podman machine ssh`
->
`vi ~/fix-host-ip.sh`
->
```
#!/bin/bash

# 1. Get the Windows Host IP (the default gateway of the WSL eth0 interface)
WINDOWS_IP=$(ip route show default | awk '{print $3}')

# 2. Define the path to your user-level containers.conf
CONF_DIR="/etc/containers"
CONF_FILE="$CONF_DIR/containers.conf"

# 3. Ensure the directory exists
mkdir -p "$CONF_DIR"

# 4. Check if the file exists; if not, create the [containers] header
if [ ! -f "$CONF_FILE" ]; then
    echo "[containers]" > "$CONF_FILE"
fi

# 5. Use sed to update the value if it exists, or append it if it doesn't
if grep -q "host_containers_internal_ip" "$CONF_FILE"; then
    # Replace existing line
    sed -i "s/^host_containers_internal_ip = .*/host_containers_internal_ip = \"$WINDOWS_IP\"/" "$CONF_FILE"
else
    # Append to the [containers] section
    sed -i "/\[containers\]/a host_containers_internal_ip = \"$WINDOWS_IP\"" "$CONF_FILE"
fi

echo "Updated host.containers.internal to: $WINDOWS_IP"
```
->
`chmod +x ~/fix-host-ip.sh`
->
`vi ~/.bashrc`
->
`~/fix-host-ip.sh > /dev/null 2>&1`

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
